name: Varanasi
tagline: The oldest living city on Earth — where death is a celebration and the Ganga burns 24/7.
why_special: Varanasi doesn't ease you in. Cremation pyres burn openly at Manikarnika Ghat, sadhus smear ash on their foreheads, and the evening Ganga Aarti at Dashashwamedh is one of the most overwhelming sensory experiences in India. Sarnath, where the Buddha gave his first sermon, is 10km away.
medical_facility: Multiple hospitals including BHU hospital (one of the largest in UP)

deep_dive.detailed_description: Varanasi (Kashi/Banaras) is claimed to be the oldest continuously inhabited city in the world — 3000+ years. The 84 ghats along the western bank of the Ganga define the city. The narrow galis (alleys) behind the ghats are an impossibly dense maze of temples, silk shops, chai stalls, and life itself. It's not pretty. It's not clean. It is absolutely unforgettable.
things_to_do: Dawn boat ride on the Ganga — watch the ghats wake up
Evening Ganga Aarti at Dashashwamedh Ghat (arrive 30 min early for front seats)
Walk the ghats from Assi to Manikarnika
Sarnath — Dhamek Stupa, museum, Ashoka Pillar
Banarasi silk shopping in Vishwanath Gali (bargain hard)
Kashi Vishwanath Temple (new corridor makes it accessible)
Ramnagar Fort across the river
deep_dive.access_notes: Direct flights from Delhi, Mumbai, Bangalore. Well-connected by train — Varanasi Junction and Manduadih are the main stations. Auto-rickshaws for transport; ghats area is walking-only.
deep_dive.stays: Brijrama Palace (heritage, on the ghat), Suryauday Haveli, budget guesthouses in Assi Ghat area. Ghat-facing rooms are worth the premium — the sunrise view is the point.
deep_dive.food: Kachori-sabzi at Ram Bhandar (breakfast institution since 1925), chaat at Deena Chaat Bhandar, Banarasi paan at every corner, thandai (legal version, no bhang unless you ask), malaiyo in winter (frothy saffron milk dessert, seasonal Nov-Feb only), lassi at Blue Lassi.
deep_dive.festivals: Dev Deepawali (Nov — million diyas on ghats, spectacular), Mahashivratri (Feb/Mar — massive), Ganga Mahotsav (Nov). Holi is wild here.
deep_dive.photography: Dawn boat ride (the light on the ghats is legendary), Manikarnika cremation ghat (respectful distance, no close-ups), evening aarti fire ceremony, narrow gali life, silk weavers at work
deep_dive.hidden_gems: Tulsi Manas Mandir (entire Ramayana on the walls), Bharat Kala Bhavan museum at BHU, the silk weaver homes in Muslim quarter (ask for a loom demonstration)
deep_dive.history: Referenced in the Rigveda. Mark Twain called it 'older than history, older than tradition, older even than legend.' Continuous habitation for 3000+ years. Every major Indian religion has roots here.
deep_dive.warnings: Extremely crowded and chaotic. Touts are aggressive near the ghats — ignore anyone who 'helpfully' guides you. Cremation ghats: watch from a respectful distance, do NOT photograph close-up. Summer (Apr-Jun) is 42-47°C and unbearable. Monsoon floods the lower ghats.

monthly_suitability.note: Peak winter season. Cool (8-20°C), clear skies, malaiyo season. Best time.
Still cool and pleasant. Mahashivratri usually falls here — massive but manageable.
Warming up (20-32°C). Holi is extraordinary here but very intense. Good otherwise.
Getting hot (30-40°C). Still doable early mornings and evenings.
Brutal heat. 42-47°C. Ghats are a furnace. Do not visit unless you have no choice.
Pre-monsoon heat + humidity. 45°C+ possible. Miserable.
Monsoon arrives. Lower ghats flood, Ganga in spate. Humidity unbearable but temperatures drop slightly.
Heavy monsoon continues. Flooding common. Green surroundings but sticky weather.
Monsoon receding. Still warm and humid but improving.
Post-monsoon sweet spot. Comfortable 25-33°C. Crowds building for festival season.
Dev Deepawali (million diyas on ghats). Best festival month. Perfect weather.
Cool, misty mornings. Ganga Aarti in winter fog is atmospheric. Peak tourist season.

reasons: Boat ride on the Ganga is magical for all ages
Sarnath is educational and spacious — good for kids
Evening aarti is a spectacle kids remember forever
concerns: Cremation ghats require explaining death to children — can be confronting
Extremely crowded narrow lanes — easy to lose a child
Summer heat is dangerous for children
Aggressive touts and chaotic traffic near ghats
Hygiene standards in old city are very low
kid_highlights: Dawn boat ride — spotting turtles and dolphins in the Ganga
Sarnath deer park and museum
Watching the aarti fire ceremony
Lassi at Blue Lassi (kids love choosing flavours)

name: Agra
tagline: The city is rough. The Taj is worth every second of it.
why_special: The Taj Mahal is not overhyped — it genuinely stops you in your tracks. Agra Fort is arguably more impressive architecturally (you can see the Taj from Shah Jahan's prison window). Fatehpur Sikri, 40km away, is a ghost city of Mughal genius. Three UNESCO sites in one district.
medical_facility: Multiple hospitals including SN Medical College

deep_dive.detailed_description: Agra was the Mughal capital for 130 years (1526-1658). The city today is noisy, polluted, and aggressively touristy — but it contains three UNESCO World Heritage Sites within a 40km radius. The Taj Mahal, Agra Fort, and Fatehpur Sikri are non-negotiable for any India trip. The Yamuna Expressway from Delhi has cut travel time to 3 hours.
things_to_do: Taj Mahal at sunrise (enter from East Gate, arrive 30 min before opening)
Mehtab Bagh at sunset — Taj view across the Yamuna without the crowds
Agra Fort — Shah Jahan's prison with Taj view, Diwan-i-Khas, Jahangir's Palace
Fatehpur Sikri — Buland Darwaza, Panch Mahal, ghost city vibes (40km)
Itmad-ud-Daulah (Baby Taj) — the marble inlay prototype for the Taj
Marble inlay shopping in Taj Nagari area (watch artisans at work)
deep_dive.access_notes: Gatimaan Express from Delhi (1h40m, fastest). Yamuna Expressway by car (3h). Can be done as a Delhi day trip but 2 nights is better to avoid rushing. Agra Cantt is the main station.
deep_dive.stays: Oberoi Amarvilas (Taj view from every room, splurge), ITC Mughal, budget options on Fatehabad Road. Taj-view rooms are worth the premium for sunrise.
deep_dive.food: Petha (Agra's famous sweet — Panchhi Petha is the OG), Bedai-jalebi breakfast at Deviram, Mughlai food at Pinch of Spice, chaat at Sadar Bazaar. Food scene is decent but not destination-worthy like Lucknow.
deep_dive.festivals: Taj Mahotsav (Feb — 10-day cultural festival near Taj), Urs at Fatehpur Sikri Dargah
deep_dive.photography: Taj at sunrise from inside (the marble changes colour), Mehtab Bagh sunset silhouette, Agra Fort's Musamman Burj (Shah Jahan's view), Fatehpur Sikri's Panch Mahal geometry, Itmad-ud-Daulah detail work
deep_dive.hidden_gems: Akbar's Tomb at Sikandra (surprisingly uncrowded for an emperor's tomb), Ram Bagh (oldest Mughal garden in India), Chambal river boat safari from Agra (2 hours south — gharials, dolphins)
deep_dive.history: Founded as Akbar's capital, peaked under Shah Jahan who built the Taj (1632-1653) as a tomb for Mumtaz Mahal. 20,000 workers, 22 years, white Makrana marble. Shah Jahan was later imprisoned by his own son Aurangzeb in Agra Fort with only a Taj view for company.
deep_dive.warnings: Scams are relentless — fake guides, gem shops, 'my uncle's marble factory.' Use only ASI-authorized guides. Pollution is bad. The city beyond the monuments is grimy. Taj is closed on Fridays. Don't buy 'marble' from street vendors — it's usually soapstone.

monthly_suitability.note: Cool (7-22°C), misty mornings, Taj in fog is ethereal. Peak tourist season.
Warming slightly. Taj Mahotsav festival. Excellent visiting weather.
Getting warm (15-32°C). Still comfortable for morning Taj visits.
Hot (25-38°C). Mornings still OK. Afternoon outdoor sightseeing is tough.
Scorching. 42-47°C. Marble at the Taj burns your feet. Genuinely dangerous heat.
Pre-monsoon heat. 45°C+. Dehydration risk is real. Do not plan outdoor sightseeing.
Monsoon. Cooler but very humid. Yamuna floods. Some atmospheric photos possible.
Heavy monsoon continues. Flooding around Taj possible.
Monsoon receding. Warm and humid but greenery around monuments is lush.
Post-monsoon. Clear skies, comfortable 25-33°C. Excellent light for photography.
Perfect weather. Cool mornings, clear days. Crowds building.
Cold mornings (5-20°C) but clear. Fog can delay trains but Taj-in-fog photos are iconic.

reasons: The Taj Mahal genuinely amazes children — it's in every textbook and then you're standing in front of it
Agra Fort has enough space and stories to keep kids engaged
Short trip duration (1-2 days) is perfect for kids' attention spans
Easy day trip from Delhi — no complex logistics
concerns: Summer heat is dangerous for children — avoid Apr-Jun entirely
Aggressive touts outside Taj can be stressful
Fatehpur Sikri has a lot of walking in open sun
kid_highlights: Taj Mahal — the 'is this real?' moment when they first see it
Agra Fort — pretending to be Mughal emperors
Fatehpur Sikri — exploring the ghost city
Watching marble inlay artisans at work
Petha tasting — kids love the sweet varieties

name: Lucknow
tagline: India's politest city and its greatest food city. The kebabs alone are worth the flight.
why_special: Lucknow is where Mughal refinement survived longest. The Nawabi culture created a city obsessed with tehzeeb (etiquette) and food. Tunday Kababi has been making the same galawati kebab since 1905 — it literally melts on your tongue. The Bara Imambara is one of India's largest unsupported structures. And the biryani here fights Hyderabad for the crown.
medical_facility: KGMU, Sahara Hospital, multiple major hospitals

deep_dive.detailed_description: Lucknow was the capital of the Awadh Nawabs — a dynasty that prized poetry, music, cuisine, and manners above military conquest. The result is a city where the food culture is unmatched in North India. The old city around Chowk and Aminabad is where the magic is — Chikankari embroidery shops, kebab stalls that have been family-run for generations, and crumbling Nawabi architecture.
things_to_do: Kebab trail: Tunday Kababi (Aminabad, the original), Idris biryani (Chowk), Rahim's kulcha-nihari
Bara Imambara + Bhool Bhulaiya (the labyrinth on the roof — genuinely confusing)
Chota Imambara (The Palace of Lights)
Rumi Darwaza — Lucknow's iconic gateway, modeled on Istanbul's Sublime Porte
British Residency — 1857 rebellion site, still has cannonball marks
Chikankari shopping in Chowk (white-on-white embroidery, Lucknow's signature craft)
Hazratganj for modern shopping and kulfi-falooda
deep_dive.access_notes: Direct flights from all major cities. Well-connected trains. City is spread out — use Ola/Uber or e-rickshaws in old city. Charbagh station itself is an architectural gem.
deep_dive.stays: Taj Mahal Lucknow (heritage wing), Lebua Lucknow, budget options on Vidhan Sabha Marg. The city isn't a hotel destination — stay clean and central, spend on food instead.
deep_dive.food: This IS the reason to come. Tunday Kababi galawati kebabs (original in Aminabad, tourist branch in Chowk), Idris ki biryani (Chowk — go early, sells out by 1pm), Rahim's kulcha-nihari (breakfast), Prakash ki kulfi, Sharmaji ki chai, basket chaat at Royal Cafe, Makhan malai in winter (the frothiest, lightest dessert you'll ever eat).
deep_dive.festivals: Lucknow Mahotsav (Nov-Dec, cultural extravaganza), Muharram processions (significant — Lucknow has the largest Shia population in India)
deep_dive.photography: Bara Imambara's vast hall, Rumi Darwaza at dusk, Chowk market chaos, kebab stalls at night, Chikankari artisans at work, British Residency ruins
deep_dive.hidden_gems: La Martiniere College (one of the most stunning colonial buildings in India, sometimes allows visits), Constantia (the building it's housed in), Kaiserbagh Palace ruins, old Chowk at night for the full street food experience
deep_dive.history: Awadh Nawabs ruled from 1722-1856. They transformed Lucknow into a cultural capital rivaling Delhi and Agra. The 1857 Siege of Lucknow was one of the defining moments of the Indian rebellion against the British.
deep_dive.warnings: Old city is chaotic but not dangerous. Vegetarians will struggle in the traditional food trail (it's a meat city). Summer heat (Apr-Jun, 42-47°C) makes outdoor sightseeing miserable. Some kebab shops are cash-only.

monthly_suitability.note: Cool (6-20°C). Perfect walking weather for the old city food trail. Makhan malai season.
Slightly warmer. Still excellent. Lucknow Mahotsav sometimes extends into Feb.
Warming up (18-32°C). Comfortable mornings and evenings for food walks.
Hot (28-40°C). Old city becomes an oven. Only worth it if you're a die-hard foodie.
Brutal heat 42-46°C. No shade in old city. Avoid.
Pre-monsoon furnace + humidity. Genuinely unpleasant.
Monsoon. Cooler but old city lanes flood. Street food hygiene drops.
Still monsoon. Waterlogging in old areas common.
Monsoon receding. Humid but temperatures dropping. Food stalls back to normal.
Post-monsoon. Perfect 25-33°C. Dussehra celebrations are big here.
Best month. Cool evenings, festival season, Lucknow Mahotsav. Makhan malai starts.
Cold mornings, clear days. Peak food tourism season. Kebabs taste better in the cold.

reasons: Bhool Bhulaiya labyrinth at Bara Imambara — kids absolutely love getting lost in it
Food trail is an adventure even for picky eaters (kebabs are universal)
City is relatively safe and people are genuinely polite
Flat terrain — no exhausting climbs
concerns: Old city is chaotic and crowded — hold hands
Summer heat is dangerous for children
Most famous food is non-vegetarian — vegetarian kids will miss the highlight
Street food hygiene varies — stick to established names
kid_highlights: Bhool Bhulaiya maze — the rooftop labyrinth is a natural playground
Trying galawati kebabs at Tunday Kababi
E-rickshaw ride through Chowk bazaar
Kulfi-falooda at Prakash
Chikankari shopping — watching artisans embroider

name: Vrindavan & Mathura
tagline: Where Krishna grew up, where Holi is a full-contact sport, and where monkeys rule.
why_special: Mathura is Krishna's birthplace. Vrindavan is where he played as a child. Together they form the spiritual heart of the Braj region. Prem Mandir at night is jaw-dropping — white marble lit up like a jewel. And if you come for Holi (Feb/Mar), Braj's Lathmar Holi, flower Holi, and colour Holi across multiple days is unlike anything else on the planet.
medical_facility: District hospital in Mathura, private hospitals available

deep_dive.detailed_description: The twin cities of Mathura and Vrindavan (12km apart) are the epicenter of Krishna worship. Vrindavan has 5000+ temples, from ancient ones to the massive ISKCON temple and the spectacular Prem Mandir. The Braj region's Holi celebrations run for a week+ across multiple towns — Barsana (Lathmar Holi, where women beat men with sticks), Nandgaon, Vrindavan (flower Holi at Banke Bihari), and Mathura (colour Holi).
things_to_do: Prem Mandir at night — illuminated white marble, free entry, breathtaking
Krishna Janmabhoomi Temple (Mathura) — the exact birthplace
Banke Bihari Temple — the most intense darshan experience, crowds surge
ISKCON Temple — well-organized, good for first-timers
Yamuna Aarti at Keshi Ghat (Vrindavan's answer to Varanasi)
Holi across Braj — Lathmar Holi in Barsana (1 week before main Holi)
deep_dive.access_notes: 2.5 hours from Delhi by road (Yamuna Expressway via Agra turn-off). Mathura Junction is well-connected. Easy to combine with Agra (60km). Local transport: e-rickshaws and shared autos.
deep_dive.stays: ISKCON guesthouse (clean, vegetarian, strict timings), MVT Guesthouse, basic hotels in Vrindavan. Mathura has slightly better hotel options. Don't expect luxury — this is a pilgrimage town.
deep_dive.food: Strictly vegetarian city (no meat, no eggs available). Peda from Mathura (the original milk sweet), rabri, lassi, puri-sabzi at temple langars. Brijwasi is the famous peda brand.
deep_dive.festivals: Holi (Feb/Mar — the main event, plan months ahead for accommodation), Janmashtami (Aug/Sep — Krishna's birthday, midnight celebrations), Kartik month (Oct/Nov — oil lamp festivals)
deep_dive.photography: Prem Mandir illuminated at night, Holi colour clouds at Banke Bihari, Lathmar Holi action shots at Barsana, Yamuna aarti at Keshi Ghat, monkey troops on temple roofs
deep_dive.hidden_gems: Nidhivan (mysterious forest where Krishna is said to dance at night — locked after sunset), Radha Vallabh Temple (intimate, ancient), Govardhan Hill parikrama (21km — the hill Krishna lifted)
deep_dive.history: Krishna's birthplace (3228 BCE according to Hindu tradition). The Mathura school of art (2nd century CE) created some of the first Buddha and Hindu deity sculptures. Repeatedly sacked by invaders but always rebuilt — the devotion here is indestructible.
deep_dive.warnings: Monkeys are extremely aggressive — they will snatch food, glasses, phones. Keep everything in bags. Banke Bihari Temple crowds can be suffocating — avoid peak aarti if claustrophobic. During Holi, colours are permanent on clothes and can irritate skin — wear old clothes, apply coconut oil on skin before.

monthly_suitability.note: Cool (5-20°C). Pleasant temple visits. Quiet season.
Pre-Holi buzz begins. Weather perfect. Lathmar Holi in Barsana if Holi falls in March.
HOLI MONTH (usually). The reason to come. Book 2 months ahead. Warming up but manageable.
Hot (30-40°C). Post-Holi emptiness. Temple visits only early morning.
Scorching 42-46°C. Stone temples radiate heat. Miserable.
Pre-monsoon hell. 45°C+. Even locals avoid midday.
Monsoon. Cooler but Yamuna floods, roads waterlog. Temples accessible.
Janmashtami (if it falls here) is massive — midnight celebrations, city comes alive.
Post-monsoon. Warm but bearable. Crowds moderate.
Kartik month lamp festivals. Cool evenings. Beautiful temple atmosphere.
Perfect weather. Festival season continues. Uncrowded compared to Holi.
Cold mornings, pleasant days. Good time for unhurried temple visits.

reasons: Krishna stories are inherently kid-friendly — butter thief, playful god
Prem Mandir is visually stunning and spacious enough for kids to enjoy
Holi is the most fun festival in the world for adventurous kids
ISKCON temple is well-organized and manageable for families
concerns: Monkeys WILL target children carrying food — brief kids before visiting
Banke Bihari Temple crowds are too intense for small children
Holi colours can irritate young skin and eyes — under-5s should skip the rough stuff
Summer heat makes it impossible for kids
kid_highlights: Prem Mandir light show at night
Playing Holi with colours and water (age 6+ recommended)
Feeding cows at Vrindavan gaushalas
Krishna stories come alive — every corner is a chapter
Peda tasting in Mathura

name: Prayagraj
tagline: Where three rivers meet, where the largest human gathering on Earth happens, and where India's independence was planned.
why_special: Triveni Sangam — the confluence of the Ganga, Yamuna, and the mythical Saraswati — is one of Hinduism's holiest points. During Kumbh Mela (every 12 years, next in 2025), 100+ million people gather here. Outside Kumbh, it's a quieter, historically significant city — Anand Bhawan (Nehru family home) is where much of India's freedom struggle was strategized.
medical_facility: Moti Lal Nehru Medical College, multiple hospitals

deep_dive.detailed_description: Prayagraj (renamed from Allahabad in 2018) sits at the exact confluence of the Ganga and Yamuna rivers, with the mythical Saraswati believed to join underground. This Triveni Sangam is considered the holiest bathing spot in Hinduism. The Kumbh Mela held here is the largest peaceful gathering in human history — visible from space. The city also has deep connections to India's independence: Anand Bhawan was the Nehru family residence, and the Indian National Congress held key sessions here.
things_to_do: Boat ride to Triveni Sangam — see the distinct colours of the two rivers meeting
Anand Bhawan and Swaraj Bhawan — Nehru family museums, beautifully preserved
Allahabad Fort (partially open, Akbar-era, contains underground Patalpuri Temple)
Khusro Bagh — Mughal gardens with stunning tombs (surprisingly unknown)
Civil Lines area — colonial-era architecture, Allahabad University campus
deep_dive.access_notes: Direct trains from Delhi, Varanasi, Lucknow. Bamrauli airport has limited flights. Easy to combine with Varanasi (130km, 2.5 hours). During Kumbh Mela, special infrastructure is set up but transport is chaos — plan well ahead.
deep_dive.stays: Hotel Kanha Shyam, Civil Lines area for decent options. During Kumbh, tent cities are set up with varying comfort levels. Outside Kumbh, hotel options are limited but adequate.
deep_dive.food: Chaat at Loknath (famous across UP), kachori at Netram, lassi at Hari Ram, puri-sabzi at Civil Lines stalls. Not a food destination like Lucknow or Varanasi, but solid street food.
deep_dive.festivals: Kumbh Mela (every 12 years — next Maha Kumbh was 2025), Magh Mela (annual, Jan-Feb at Sangam), Ardh Kumbh (every 6 years)
deep_dive.photography: Sangam boat shots (two river colours merging), Kumbh Mela crowds (once in a lifetime), Khusro Bagh tombs, Anand Bhawan interiors, dawn bathers at the confluence
deep_dive.hidden_gems: Khusro Bagh (Mughal tombs in a walled garden — barely any tourists), Allahabad Museum (good collection of Gandharan art), University campus colonial architecture walk
deep_dive.history: Known as Prayag in ancient texts — one of the four sites of the Kumbh Mela. Akbar built the fort here in 1583. Became Allahabad under the Mughals. Epicenter of the Indian independence movement — the 1857 rebellion, Indian National Congress sessions, Nehru family base.
deep_dive.warnings: During Kumbh/Magh Mela, crowds are overwhelming — keep belongings secure, stay hydrated, follow police instructions. Outside festival season, the city is unremarkable and infrastructure is basic. Summer heat (Apr-Jun) is extreme. The fort is partially under army control — not fully accessible.

monthly_suitability.note: Magh Mela begins. Cool (6-20°C). Sangam bathing season. Excellent visiting weather.
Magh Mela continues. Best month. Cool, clear, spiritual atmosphere at Sangam.
Warming (18-32°C). Mela winding down. Still comfortable for sightseeing.
Hot (28-40°C). Sangam area is exposed with no shade.
Scorching 42-46°C. Concrete city with no respite. Avoid.
Pre-monsoon heat. Unbearable 45°C+.
Monsoon. Rivers in spate — Sangam area may be inaccessible. Flooding possible.
Heavy monsoon. Both rivers swollen. Not ideal.
Monsoon receding. Rivers still high but weather improving.
Post-monsoon. Comfortable 25-33°C. Sangam accessible again. Quiet and pleasant.
Cool, clear. Perfect for Sangam visit and historical sightseeing.
Cool mornings. Pre-Magh Mela atmosphere building. Excellent time.

reasons: Boat ride to Sangam is exciting for kids
Anand Bhawan is an excellent history lesson — Nehru's room, letters, personal items
Short visit (1-2 days) keeps it manageable
concerns: Kumbh/Magh Mela crowds are too intense for children — not recommended with kids under 10
City infrastructure is basic — not many kid-friendly amenities
Summer heat is dangerous
Limited food variety for picky eaters
kid_highlights: Boat ride to see two rivers merge (the colour difference is visible)
Anand Bhawan — walking through Nehru's childhood home
Khusro Bagh gardens — space to run around

name: Ayodhya
tagline: Ram's city, reborn. The biggest religious tourism transformation India has seen since Varanasi's ghat makeover.
why_special: The Ram Mandir, consecrated in January 2024, has turned Ayodhya from a sleepy pilgrimage town into India's fastest-growing religious tourism destination. The temple itself is massive — Nagara-style architecture in pink sandstone. The Saryu river ghats have been renovated. The city is mid-transformation: new airport, new roads, new hotels, and millions of visitors where there used to be thousands.
medical_facility: District hospital, new medical facilities being built

deep_dive.detailed_description: Ayodhya is believed to be the birthplace of Lord Ram. The Ram Mandir consecration in January 2024 was a watershed moment — within months, the city went from receiving a few thousand visitors daily to lakhs. The temple is built on the disputed site, with the idol of Ram Lalla at its center. Beyond the temple, Ayodhya has the renovated Saryu ghats (7km stretch), ancient temples like Hanuman Garhi, and a rapidly developing tourism infrastructure.
things_to_do: Ram Mandir darshan (expect queues of 2-4 hours on weekends; weekday mornings are best)
Saryu river ghat walk — newly renovated, evening aarti
Hanuman Garhi temple (hilltop, panoramic city views)
Kanak Bhawan (ornate temple, less crowded)
Ram ki Paidi ghats (beautifully lit at night)
Boat ride on the Saryu at sunset
deep_dive.access_notes: New airport opened 2024 with flights from Delhi, Mumbai, Bangalore. Good train connectivity. City is compact — autos and e-rickshaws. Infrastructure is still catching up with visitor numbers — expect construction and dust.
deep_dive.stays: Hotel scene is transforming rapidly. Ramada, Sarovar, new budget hotels mushrooming. Book well ahead for weekends and festivals. Dharamshalas available for budget stays.
deep_dive.food: Strictly vegetarian city. Puri-sabzi and kachori for breakfast, Ram Rasoi (temple langar — free, sattvic food), pedas and laddoos. Not a food destination — eat simple, focus on the experience.
deep_dive.festivals: Ram Navami (Mar/Apr — the biggest day, millions gather), Diwali (the city lights up spectacularly as Ram's homecoming), Chhath Puja (Oct/Nov), Kartik Purnima
deep_dive.photography: Ram Mandir at golden hour (no phones/cameras inside the garbhagriha), Saryu ghats illuminated at night, Hanuman Garhi overlooking the city, evening aarti
deep_dive.hidden_gems: Gulab Bari (Mughal-era rose garden and tomb — nearly unknown), Treta ke Thakur temple (ancient, peaceful), Nageshwarnath Temple (said to be founded by Kush, Ram's son)
deep_dive.history: One of the seven sacred cities (Sapta Puri) of Hinduism. Believed to be the capital of the Kosala kingdom ruled by Ram. The site of the Ram Mandir has been contested for centuries — the Supreme Court verdict in 2019 cleared the way for construction. The consecration in January 2024 was watched by hundreds of millions.
deep_dive.warnings: Weekends and festivals see massive crowds — plan for long queues. Infrastructure is actively being built — roads may be blocked, dust is constant. Security is very tight around the temple. No electronics allowed inside the main temple. City is dry (no alcohol). Summer heat is extreme.

monthly_suitability.note: Anniversary of consecration. Cool (8-20°C). Heavy crowds around January 22.
Cool and pleasant. Manageable crowds on weekdays.
Warming up. Ram Navami (if it falls here) brings millions — plan or avoid accordingly.
Ram Navami month (usually). Hot (30-40°C) but the festival atmosphere is electric.
Scorching 42-46°C. Standing in queue for hours in this heat is brutal. Avoid.
Pre-monsoon heat. 45°C+. Genuinely dangerous for outdoor queuing.
Monsoon brings some relief. Saryu in spate. Roads can waterlog.
Monsoon continues. Humid but cooler than May-June.
Monsoon receding. Getting comfortable. Crowds moderate.
Post-monsoon. Perfect weather. Diwali (if it falls here) is magical — the city of lights.
Diwali/Kartik Purnima. Cool evenings, lit-up ghats. Spectacular.
Cool, clear, festive. Building toward January anniversary. Excellent time.

reasons: Ram Mandir is visually spectacular and the Ramayana story resonates with kids
Saryu ghats are spacious and well-maintained — safe for families
Short trip (1-2 days) keeps it manageable
Boat ride on Saryu is enjoyable for all ages
concerns: Queue times of 2-4 hours are exhausting for children
No electronics inside temple — kids can't be distracted
Summer heat makes queuing dangerous for children
City infrastructure still developing — footpaths, traffic management patchy
Very crowded on weekends — stressful with small children
kid_highlights: Ram Mandir — the scale and architecture impress even young children
Boat ride on the Saryu river
Hanuman Garhi — climbing up to the hilltop temple
Evening ghat illumination — kids love the lights
